rules.guidance: A short film is strongest when the audience can track one clear pressure from beginning to end.
rules.beginner_example: Instead of adding family history, work problems, and romance, focus on whether the father can say goodbye before his daughter leaves.

rules.guidance: Choose the main emotional shift and build the film around it.
rules.beginner_example: A character moves from pride to vulnerability, or from denial to acceptance.

rules.guidance: Too many characters dilute attention and make production harder.
rules.beginner_example: A father, daughter, and one supporting character may be enough.

rules.guidance: Fewer locations make the story easier to produce and can make the film feel more focused.
rules.beginner_example: A home, a taxi, and a bus station may be stronger than ten scattered places.

rules.rule: Every scene should move the story.
rules.guidance: Each scene should change information, emotion, pressure, or the character's choices.
rules.beginner_example: A phone call should not only explain the situation; it should make the father decide whether to act.

rules.guidance: Use behavior, images, silence, and objects before relying on explanation.
rules.beginner_example: Show the father folding his daughter's childhood drawing instead of saying he misses her childhood.

rules.guidance: Begin close to the moment when the story pressure becomes unavoidable.
rules.beginner_example: Start when the daughter is already leaving tonight, not months before.

rules.rule: End as soon as the emotional turn lands.
rules.guidance: A short film should not overstay after the main choice or realization.
rules.beginner_example: End on the daughter listening to the father's apology rather than showing a long aftermath.

rules.rule: Give the protagonist a visible goal.
rules.guidance: The audience should be able to see what the character is trying to do.
rules.beginner_example: The father wants to reach the station before departure.

rules.rule: Give the protagonist an emotional need.
rules.guidance: The visible goal should connect to an inner change or realization.
rules.beginner_example: The father needs to stop controlling the goodbye and speak honestly.

rules.guidance: Specific obstacles create better scenes than vague difficulty.
rules.beginner_example: A broken phone and a missed bus are more filmable than 'life gets complicated.'

rules.guidance: Short films often work best when the climax reveals character through a decision.
rules.beginner_example: The father chooses to apologize instead of begging his daughter to stay.

rules.guidance: A deadline can give a short film urgency without adding extra plot.
rules.beginner_example: The daughter's flight leaves in two hours.

rules.guidance: Backstory should appear only when it changes the present moment.
rules.beginner_example: Show an old photo because it affects the father's choice, not because the audience needs a full history lesson.

rules.guidance: Props can express emotion in a compact, cinematic way.
rules.beginner_example: A repaired watch, ticket stub, or packed suitcase can say more than a speech.

rules.guidance: Silence can reveal tension, avoidance, grief, or love when paired with clear behavior.
rules.beginner_example: The father and daughter sit together without speaking before he finally hands her a note.

rules.guidance: The theme should emerge through choices and images, not lectures.
rules.beginner_example: Instead of saying 'letting go is love,' show the father stepping back as his daughter boards.

rules.guidance: A strong final image can make a short film memorable.
rules.beginner_example: The father watches the bus leave while holding the unopened gift he decided not to force on her.

rules.guidance: Dialogue is stronger when characters want something from each other.
rules.beginner_example: A goodbye conversation becomes dramatic if one person wants honesty and the other wants escape.

rules.guidance: Coincidences can weaken the feeling that characters are making meaningful choices.
rules.beginner_example: Do not solve the story by having the daughter randomly return; make the father choose how to reach her.

rules.guidance: A scene should end in a different emotional or practical place than it began.
rules.beginner_example: A simple taxi ride becomes a turning point when the father admits why he is afraid to call.

rules.rule: Keep the world just big enough.
rules.guidance: A short film can suggest a larger world without showing all of it.
rules.beginner_example: One apartment room can reveal an entire family history through what is missing from the walls.

rules.guidance: Limitations can help focus the story if used intentionally.
rules.beginner_example: If you only have one location, make the conflict unfold through calls, objects, and arrivals at the door.

rules.guidance: Short films usually need emotional clarity more than complicated plot surprises.
rules.beginner_example: One reveal about why the father hesitated is enough; three reversals may feel rushed.

rules.guidance: Find the scene or image with the most emotional power and shape the film to support it.
rules.beginner_example: If the strongest moment is the father recording an apology, build the story toward that action.
